Many people followed Christ. It is best for us to follow him when he goes away. They found him able and ready to help elsewhere, just as he had been in Galilee; wherever the Sun of Righteousness appeared, it brought healing with it.

The Pharisees wanted to find something in Jesus’ words that they could present as being against the law of Moses. There have been many cases about marriage, and they have sometimes caused confusion; this was not because of God's law, but because of people’s desires and mistakes. People often decide what they will do before seeking advice. Jesus responded by asking if they had not read the account of creation and the first example of marriage; in doing so, he pointed out that any departure from that condition was wrong. The best condition for us is one that we choose and maintain to prepare us for and preserve us in the kingdom of heaven. When the gospel is truly embraced, it makes people kind relatives and faithful friends; it teaches them to bear burdens and endure weaknesses of those they are connected with, and to value their peace and happiness more than their own. Ungodly people should be restrained by laws from disturbing public order. We learn that marriage should be entered into with great seriousness and earnest prayer.

It is good when we come to Christ ourselves and bring our children. Little children can be brought to Christ as those who need and are able to receive blessings from him, and have an interest in his intercession. We can only ask for a blessing for them; Christ alone can grant the blessing. It is good that Christ has more love and tenderness than even his best disciples. Let us learn from him not to discourage any willing, well-meaning souls seeking after Christ, even if they are weak. Those who are given to Christ as part of his purchase will never be cast out by him. Therefore, he takes it badly when anyone forbids or tries to shut out those whom he has received. All Christians should bring their children to the Savior that he may bless them with spiritual blessings.

Christ knew that covetousness was a sin that easily tempted this young man; although he had honestly acquired what he possessed, he could not cheerfully part with it, and this revealed his lack of sincerity. Christ’s promises make his commands easy and his yoke pleasant and comfortable; yet this promise was as much a test of the young man's faith as the command was of his charity and rejection of worldly things. Following Christ requires that we attend his teachings, follow his example, and cheerfully submit to his will—all from love for him and dependence on him. Selling everything and giving to the poor is not enough; we must follow Christ. The gospel is the only solution for lost sinners. Many abstain from obvious sins while neglecting their obligations to God. Thousands of instances of disobedience in thought, word, and deed are recorded against them in the book of God. Thus, many people forsake Christ, loving this present world; they feel convictions and desires but depart sorrowful, perhaps trembling. We must examine ourselves in these matters, for the Lord will examine us.

Although Christ spoke so strongly, few who have wealth do not trust in it. How few who are poor are not tempted to envy! But people’s eagerness in this matter is like their efforts to build a high wall to shut themselves and their children out of heaven. It should be comforting for those in low circumstances that they are not exposed to the temptations of a wealthy condition. If they live more harshly in this world than the rich, but if they reach a better world more easily, they have no reason to complain. Christ’s words show that it is difficult for a rich person to be a good Christian and to be saved. The way to heaven is narrow for everyone, and the gate leading into it is restricted; especially so for wealthy people. More duties are expected of them than others, and more sins easily tempt them. It is hard not to be charmed by a pleasant world. Rich people have much to account for because of their opportunities compared to others. It is utterly impossible for someone who sets his heart on his riches to reach heaven. Christ used an expression indicating a difficulty that cannot be overcome by human power. Nothing less than God’s almighty grace will enable a rich person to overcome this difficulty. Then, who can be saved? the disciples asked. No one, Christ said, by any created power. The beginning, progress, and completion of salvation depend entirely on the almighty power of God, to which all things are possible. It is not that rich people can be saved in their worldliness, but that they should be saved from it. Peter said, We have forsaken everything. Alas! it was only a poor amount—a few boats and nets; yet observe how Peter speaks as if it had been something significant. We are too quick to exaggerate our services and sufferings, our expenses and losses for Christ. However, Christ does not reproach them; although they gave little, it was their all, and as dear to them as if it had been more. Christ accepted that they left it to follow him; he accepts according to what a person has. The Lord’s promise to the apostles is that when the Son of man sits on his throne of glory, he will make all things new, and they will sit with him in judgment on those who are judged by their teachings. This demonstrates the honor, dignity, and authority of their office and ministry. Our Lord added that everyone who had forsaken possessions or comforts for his sake and the gospel would be rewarded at last. May God give us faith to rest our hope on this promise; then we will be ready for every service or sacrifice. Our Savior, in the final verse, corrects a misunderstanding of some. The heavenly inheritance is not given as earthly ones are, but according to God’s pleasure. Let us not trust in promising appearances or outward professions. Others may, for all we know, become eminent in faith and holiness.
